macro_segments.title: Introduction to Shelichus and the Source Texts (Terumah, Kiddushin, Gittin, Korbanos)

micro_segments.title: Overview of the daf and wrap-up of shelichus sources

macro_segments.title: Exceptions to Shelichus: The Sending of Fire Through Unresponsible Persons (Cheresh, Shoteh, Katan vs. Pikeach)

micro_segments.title: The case of sending fire through deaf-mute, mentally ill, or minor versus responsible adult
Explanation why the meshaleach is not liable when a responsible adult commits the act
The principle of ein shaliach lidvar aveira and the rule divrei harav vedivrei hatalmid
Discussion of whether this applies when the shaliach is unaware of the sin

macro_segments.title: Me'ilah (Misuse of Consecrated Property) and Shelichus: When Does the Sender Become Liable

micro_segments.title: The principle that if the shaliach fails to perform his mission, he is liable for me'ilah; if he performs it, the ba'al habayis is liable
Challenge: how can the ba'al habayis be liable if there is no shaliach lidvar aveira?
Answer using gezeirah shavah from terumah to me'ilah and the principle that two occurrences in Torah teach exceptions

macro_segments.title: Shelichus Yad (Unauthorized Use of Another's Property) as a Second Exception to Ein Shaliach Lidvar Aveira

micro_segments.title: Introduction to shelichus yad: the case of a watchman who uses another's property without permission
Two categories of exceptions: me'ilah and shelichus yad; principle that two scriptural sources indicate exceptions, not rules
Debate between Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel on whether thought alone or actual physical use constitutes shelichus yad

macro_segments.title: Tevicha Umechira (Slaughtering or Selling Stolen Livestock) as a Third Exception

micro_segments.title: The case of a thief who slaughters or sells stolen livestock; liability multiplies even if shaliach does it
Two sources for including shaliach in tevicha umechira: the word 'or' and the word 'under' (tachas)
Confirmation that these are exceptions and not rules; the paradigm from shchutei chutz (slaughtering outside the Temple)

macro_segments.title: The General Rule: Ein Shaliach Lidvar Aveira, Confirmed by Shchutei Chutz Paradigm

micro_segments.title: Use of shchutei chutz as the paradigm case establishing ein shaliach lidvar aveira as the rule
The double 'hahu' in the pasuk as additional support that only the doer is liable, not the shaliach
For those holding shnei kesuvim habaim k'echad ein melamdin, the other uses of double hahu (shenanayim, yichashev, hu velo shluco)

macro_segments.title: Shammai HaZaken's Position: Shaliach Lidvar Aveira in Murder (Asumo Haragta B'Cherev B'nei Amon)

micro_segments.title: Case where one tells a shaliach to commit murder; standard rule is doer liable, sender exempt
Shammai HaZaken's opposing view via the pasuk about David and Uriah: sender is liable
Explanation of Shammai's position: he holds shnei kesuvim habaim k'echad melamdim, so yesh shaliach lidvar aveira
Alternative explanation: Shammai means the sender is liable only b'dinei shamayim (in Heaven), not b'dinei adam (in human courts)
Further interpretation: the case of murder may be a specific exception where the sender bears liability

macro_segments.title: Raba's Clarification: Cases Where Ein Shaliach Lidvar Aveira Even for Shammai

micro_segments.title: Raba: even Shammai holds ein shaliach lidvar aveira for cases of intercourse with forbidden woman or eating non-kosher food
Reason: the sin is not the act itself but the pleasure (hana'ah) experienced by the shaliach, which cannot be attributed to the sender

macro_segments.title: Shaliach Na'aseh Eid: Can a Shaliach Serve as a Witness to His Own Mission

micro_segments.title: Initial debate: Rav holds shaliach na'aseh eid (can be a witness); Beis Rabbi Shila holds ein shaliach na'aseh eid (cannot)
Challenge from baraita: Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel both seem to hold ein shaliach na'aseh eid in kiddushin case
Resolution: reversal of baraita positions; Beis Hillel holds shaliach cannot be eid, but Beis Shammai holds shaliach can be eid
Conclusion via Rav Acha bar Rava and Raba: the halacha is shaliach na'aseh eid

macro_segments.title: Application of Shaliach Na'aseh Eid to Kiddushin, Gerushin, and Dinei Mamonot

micro_segments.title: Rule: if you send two people to marry a woman, they are both shluchim and both can serve as eidim
Same applies to gerushin (divorce) with a caveat: concern that he might want to marry her himself
Same applies to dinei mamonot (monetary matters) with caveat: concern that they might pocket the money
Explanation: concerns are addressed because for kiddushin, woman becomes forbidden; for gerushin and mamonot, two witnesses cannot both benefit equally

macro_segments.title: The Impact of Shevuas Hesess on Shaliach Witnesses in Monetary Cases

micro_segments.title: Before shevuas hesess: shluchim could serve as eidim in monetary matters because they had no gain from testifying (malveh et chaveiro b'eidim)
After shevuas hesess introduced: shluchim now gain by testifying (avoiding oath to meshaleach), so they become nogea b'eidus (interested parties)
Result: shluchim can no longer serve as eidim; instead they take oath that they paid, meshaleach takes oath they didn't, and meshaleach collects from debtor
